region_of_origin: India (North India / Punjab); served widely in Singapore

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kadai Chicken is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a bowl of bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in thick tomato-based gravy, suitable as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken curry (cooked, with gravy)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kadai Chicken is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'chicken curry with gravy' (cooked, bone-in, tomato-onion base) as the closest approved-source analogue, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed' (FDC ID 171477) for raw-ingredient macro anchoring. Input per_100g values from the original record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with the ICMR-NIN adapted range and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium value (271 mg/100g) is plausible for a home-style kadai preparation with added salt and spice blend.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; analogous estimate from chicken curry with tomato-onion gravy
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kadai Chicken. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous food: 'chicken curry with tomato-onion gravy' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dish is low-carbohydrate (5.7 g/100g), protein-dominant (12.9 g/100g), and contains no high-GI starch component. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (35 × 5.7 × 3.5) / 100 = 7.0, rounded to 7.2 accounting for sugar contribution. GL is low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271 mg/100g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 949 mg sodium, representing ~41% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced added salt; the dish's potassium contribution from tomatoes, bell peppers, and onions partially offsets sodium load, but ghee or cream additions should be avoided to keep saturated fat low.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.7 g/100g), low GI (~35), high protein (12.9 g/100g) slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai Chicken is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when served without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; pair with salad or non-starchy vegetables and monitor sodium intake from the spice blend.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 2.9 g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.06 g/100g, but sodium at 271 mg/100g is a concern for cardiovascular risk at full serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is acceptable for cardiovascular health when prepared with vegetable oil rather than ghee, portion-controlled to 150–200 g, and served without cream; the anti-inflammatory spice profile (ginger, garlic, cinnamon, cardamom) provides additional cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains multiple nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers, green chillies) which may trigger joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with RA who are sensitive to nightshades should avoid or limit this dish; those who tolerate nightshades may benefit from the anti-inflammatory gingerols, allicin, and polyphenols in the spice blend.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Chicken (also spelled Kadhai Chicken or Karahi Chicken; Hindi: कड़ाई चिकन; Chinese: 铁锅鸡) is a popular North Indian chicken curry cooked in a kadai — a thick, circular, deep cooking vessel similar to a wok — over high heat. The dish features bone-in or boneless chicken pieces stir-fried with onions, ginger, garlic, and bell peppers, then simmered in a rich, semi-dry tomato-based gravy seasoned with kadai masala, a freshly ground spice blend typically comprising coriander seeds, cumin, dried red chillies, black pepper, cardamom, and cinnamon. The result is a deeply aromatic, moderately spiced curry with a thick, clingy sauce, tender chicken, and a characteristic smoky edge from high-heat wok cooking. Nutritionally, Kadai Chicken provides approximately 157 kcal per 100 g, with 12.9 g protein, 10.0 g fat (2.9 g saturated), and only 5.7 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is moderate at 271 mg/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), and a standard 350 g serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 7, suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due0 to its low GI and high protein content, and is acceptable for cardiovascular health when prepared with vegetable oil. Sodium content warrants portion control for hypertensive individuals. Nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, bell peppers) may be a concern for rheumatoid arthritis sufferers. Widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, it is commonly paired with naan, roti, or steamed rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kadai Chicken?
Is Kadai Chicken good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Kadai Chicken and Butter Chicken?
Can I eat Kadai Chicken if I have high blood pressure?

Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry with gravy as the closest approved analogue, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for macro anchoring. Input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with the adapted range. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of an exact approved-source match.

The input record incorrectly described Kadai Chicken as a 'Turkish' dish; this has been corrected to Indian (North Indian / Punjabi origin). The ethnic_tags 'Turkish', 'Kebab', 'Shawarma' from the input have been removed as they are factually incorrect for this dish. 5. The input listed three portions all with is_default: true and identical 350 g weights; consolidated to a single 350 g default serving. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database and is a forbidden source (delivery/app platform); replaced with ICMR-NIN as the approved primary source. 7. No core macro is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. 8. GI estimated at 35 from analogous University of Sydney GI Database entry for chicken curry with tomato-onion gravy; this is a low-GI food.
